title: Acute Urinary Retention
oneLiner: Sudden, painful inability to void with a palpable full bladder; most common in elderly men with BPH, but also post-op or neurogenic; missed cases cause renal damage.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man with known BPH presents to the Emergency Department with suprapubic pain and the sensation that he desperately needs to pass urine but cannot. He has not passed urine for 12 hours. On examination, his bladder is distended to the umbilicus and tender. A bladder scan shows 800 mL of urine. He is agitated and sweating.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age 72 + known BPH** → prostatic obstruction is the top cause.
- **Suprapubic pain + “need to go”** → bladder wall stretching activates stretch receptors; severe discomfort.
- **No void for 12 hours** → acute complete retention (as opposed to chronic).
- **Bladder distended to umbilicus** → palpable suprapubic mass; typical of >300 mL retained.
- **Bladder scan 800 mL** → confirms retention; post-void residual >300 mL is abnormal, >600 mL is severe.
- **Agitated, sweating** → autonomic response to pain and distension; also may signal high pressure.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Postoperative patient (e.g., hip replacement)**: After spinal anaesthesia/opioids, retention is painless because sensory blockade blunts the urge. The only clue is a distended bladder on exam or poor urine output. Always check a bladder scan in any post-op patient with low output.
2. **Neurogenic retention (e.g., diabetic autonomic neuropathy)**: Presents with chronic, painless distension and overflow incontinence. The patient may not complain of pain; instead they report constant dribbling. Exam reveals a nontender, large bladder. Post-void residual often >1 L.
3. **Medication-induced (e.g., anticholinergics in an older adult)**: Sudden onset after starting a new drug (e.g., oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressant). The patient may have no prior bladder symptoms. Suspect if retention coincides with drug start.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute urinary retention is the sudden inability to pass urine **despite a full bladder**. The bladder is distended and painful (or occasionally painless if neurogenic). It is a urological emergency.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Very common – ~10% of men over 70 experience acute retention within 5 years of BPH diagnosis. Also common post-surgery (up to 20% of hip/knee arthroplasties).
- **Stakes:** If left untreated → bladder ischemia → detrusor muscle damage → chronic retention → hydronephrosis → acute kidney injury (AKI) → permanent renal impairment.
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing anuria (no urine production) as retention (can’t void despite full bladder); catheterisation in anuria is harmful. Also missing post-obstructive diuresis which can cause life-threatening fluid/electrolyte shifts.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of acute retention:**

1. **Obstruction (most common):** Bladder neck or urethra mechanically blocked – typically by an enlarged prostate (BPH) that compresses the prostatic urethra, like a kinked hose. Once the detrusor muscle can no longer overcome the resistance, it gives way → the bladder fails to empty.

2. **Detrusor failure (underactive bladder):** The bladder muscle (detrusor) lacks the strength or nerve supply to contract. Common in diabetics (autonomic neuropathy), post-anaesthesia (opioids relax smooth muscle), or after long-standing chronic retention (muscle stretched beyond its elastic limit).

3. **Neurological:** Spinal cord injury or cauda equina syndrome disrupts the sacral micturition reflex (S2–S4). The bladder becomes areflexic – no sensation of fullness, no ability to contract.

**Mechanism -> sign:**
- **Pain** → bladder wall hypoxia from severe distension stimulates visceral afferents (via pelvic nerve). Also, prostaglandins released by stretching trigger inflammation.
- **Palpable suprapubic mass** → bladder is a muscular bag that, when distended, rises out of the pelvis and becomes percussible/dull. A bladder volume of ~400 mL is palpable suprapubically; >600 mL is obvious.
- **Agitation / sweating** → pain and distension activate the sympathetic nervous system (fight or flight) – catecholamine surge.
- **Absence of void despite urge** → obstruction prevents flow; or detrusor cannot generate pressure.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
In the classic vignette, the patient has both obstruction and detrusor fatigue – his BPH (obstruction) eventually overcomes his detrusor strength. The pain tells you the bladder is still trying to contract (sensory arc intact). A painless distended bladder suggests neurogenic or chronic decompensation.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Obstructive** | BPH, urethral stricture, bladder stone, prostate cancer, pelvic mass, phimosis (children) | “Elderly man with prostatic enlargement”; “previous urethral trauma”; “palpable stone” |
| **Neurogenic** | Spinal cord injury, cauda equina syndrome, stroke, diabetes, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s | “Painless distension + overflow incontinence”; “reduced lower limb reflexes”; “saddle anaesthesia” |
| **Pharmacologic** | Anticholinergics (e.g., oxybutynin, tricyclics), alpha-agonists (decongestants, antihistamines), opioids, benzodiazepines, calcium channel blockers | “Started new medication recently”; “post-op opioids” |
| **Infectious / Inflammatory** | Acute prostatitis, cystitis, urethritis, balanitis | “Fever + tender prostate”; “dysuria before retention” |
| **Postoperative** | General/ spinal anaesthesia, opioids, supine position, immobility, pelvic surgery | “Day 1 after surgery – poor urine output”; “patient not yet mobilising” |
| **Other** | Severe constipation (compression), pelvic hematoma, trauma, psychogenic (rare) | “Has not opened bowels for days”; “post-fall pelvic fracture” |

**Most common cause overall:** **Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)** – accounts for ~60% of acute retention in men >60.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Acute retention is **clinical** – patient cannot void + painful bladder + residual volume >300 mL. There is no formal scoring system.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Bladder scan (post-void residual)** | Volume of urine remaining after void (if patient can void) | Confirm retention; quantify severity | >300 mL = abnormal; >600 mL = significant. If patient can’t void at all, scan immediately shows distended bladder (often >800 mL) |
| **Urinalysis** | Leukocytes, nitrites, blood | Rule out infection as cause or complication | +nitrites = acute prostatitis/UTI – treat before catheter removal |
| **Serum creatinine + U&E** | Renal function | Assess for AKI from back pressure / volume depletion | Elevated Cr suggests obstruction is causing hydronephrosis or pre-renal component. Post-obstructive diuresis can drop Cr rapidly |
| **Ultrasound kidney + bladder** | Hydronephrosis, bladder wall thickness, stones | If retention recurrent or unexplained; or AKI present | Bilateral hydronephrosis = high-pressure retention needing urgent decompression |
| **Prostate-specific antigen (PSA)** | Elevated in BPH/prostate cancer | Not routine in acute retention (can rise from infection/catheterisation); helpful later | Do not check within 48h of catheter – can be falsely high |
| **CT scan** | Pelvic masses, stones, ureteric obstruction | If suspicion of malignancy or stone causing retention | Look for bladder outlet or pelvic compression |

**Pattern recognition – single finding that rules in/out:**
- **Bladder scan >300 mL + inability to void** = confirms acute retention (rule in).
- **Bladder scan empty + patient says “can’t pee”** = anuria (renal failure or obstruction of both ureters). Do not catheterise – instead check renal ultrasound.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
#### Immediate (first hour) — “Decompress the bladder”
- **Urethral catheterisation**: 14–16Fr Foley, balloon 10 mL with water. Use lignocaine gel. Clean technique unless immunocompromised (then sterile).  
  **Why:** Relieves pain, prevents renal damage.  
  **Dose of lignocaine gel:** 11 mL 2% gel intraurethrally, wait 2–3 minutes.
- **If urethral catheter impossible** (e.g., known stricture, recent prostate surgery, patient on anticoagulants): **suprapubic catheterisation** under ultrasound guidance.  
  **Why:** Avoids urethral trauma.
- **Slow or fast decompression?** Older dogma said decompress slowly if volume >1 L to avoid haematuria or hypotension. Evidence shows **no difference** in outcomes – you can drain fully immediately. However, monitor for **post-obstructive diuresis** (see complications).  
  **Pitfall:** Do **not** clamp the catheter – that risks bladder rupture and infection.
- **Pain management**: Paracetamol 1g IV/PO; consider small doses of opioid if severe pain (but avoid exacerbating retention in future).

#### Ward (24–72 hours) — “Plan for catheter removal”
- **Trial Without Catheter (TWOC)** is attempted after 24–72 h **if** the cause is reversible (e.g., post-op, drug-induced).  
  **How:** Fill bladder with 300–400 mL sterile water via catheter, then remove catheter. Patient must void voluntarily. Measure voided volume + post-void residual. If residual <100 mL, success.
- **If TWOC fails or cause is obstruction (e.g., BPH):**  
  - Start **alpha-blocker**: **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily** (or Alfuzosin 10 mg PO once daily). These relax the bladder neck / prostatic smooth muscle and improve flow.  
  - Continue catheter for 3–7 days. Have patient attempt TWOC on medication.
- **If recurrent retention despite alpha-blocker** → refer to urology for **Transurethral Resection of Prostate (TURP)**.
- **If infection present** → treat with antibiotics according to local guidelines (e.g., Trimethoprim 200 mg BD PO for 7 days for uncomplicated UTI; for acute prostatitis, Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD PO × 14–28 days). **Do not remove catheter until infection cleared.**

#### Long-term — “Prevent recurrence”
- **Lifestyle**: reduce fluid intake before bed, void regularly, treat constipation.
- **Medical therapy**: continue tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily (or combination with dutasteride 0.5 mg daily if BPH large – 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor reduces prostate volume over 6–12 months).
- **Surgical**: TURP for refractory cases; or prostatectomy if large glands or malignancy.
- **For neurogenic retention**: clean intermittent self-catheterisation (CISC) 4–6 times daily, oxybutynin to reduce bladder spasms.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not remove the catheter without a plan** (no alpha-blocker, no assessment for UTI) – high chance of re-retention.
- **Do not give a fluid bolus for high urine output post-decompression** unless patient is hypotensive – post-obstructive diuresis is usually self-limiting; fluid alone can worsen polyuria.
- **Do not ignore a painless distended bladder** – it may be chronic overflow – still requires decompression to protect kidneys.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acute Urinary Retention | Anuria (Renal / Pre-renal) | Overflow Incontinence (Chronic Retention) | Functional Incontinence (e.g., immobility) |
|---------|-------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe suprapubic | None | Mild or none | None |
| **Bladder exam** | Distended, tender, dull | Empty | Distended, nontender, dull | Normal |
| **Urine output** | None despite urge | None (patient doesn’t feel full) | Dribbling small amounts frequently | Can void but can't reach toilet |
| **Bladder scan** | >300 mL | <50 mL | >300 mL (large residual) | Normal |
| **Post-void residual** | N/A (can’t void) | N/A | >300 mL | <100 mL |
| **Creatinine** | Normal or raised (AKI) | Usually raised | May be normal early, raised late | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Patient desperate to void + inability + palpable bladder | Patient not disoriented, bladder empty | Patient unaware of bladder distension, constant leak | Patient mobile but cognitively impaired |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)**
- **How it presents**: Rising creatinine, oliguria (confusingly, patient still can’t void), volume overload. Seen if retention >24–48 h.
- **Key signs**: Flank pain if hydronephrosis, leg oedema.
- **What to do**: Catheterise immediately. Monitor urine output hourly post-decompression.

**2. Post-obstructive Diuresis**
- **How it presents**: After catheterisation, huge urine output (up to 10 L/day) due to impaired concentrating ability of tubules from back-pressure.
- **Key signs**: Polyuria, risk of hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypernatraemia.
- **What to do**: Monitor I/O 4-hourly. Replace 50% of urine output with 0.45% saline + electrolytes if output >250 mL/h for 4 h. Usually self-limiting in 48 h. **Do not overreplace** – can perpetuate diuresis.

**3. Bladder Rupture (rare, <1%)**
- **How it presents**: Sudden severe suprapubic pain, inability to pass catheter, or blood at meatus after catheter insertion. Signs of peritonism.
- **Key signs**: Elevated bladder pressure >200 cmH2O. X-ray cystogram shows extravasation.
- **What to do**: Urgent urology consult. Usually requires surgical repair if intraperitoneal.

**4. Urosepsis**
- **How it presents**: Fever, hypotension, confusion in a patient with retention and UTI.
- **Key signs**: Positive blood cultures for gram-negative rods.
- **What to do**: Catheterise + IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS). Escalate to ICU if shock.

**Late presentation pattern:**  
A patient with undiagnosed chronic retention (painless, large bladder) eventually develops bilateral hydronephrosis, presenting with uraemia (nausea, confusion, pruritus) and a non-tender distended bladder. This is “high-pressure chronic retention” – needs urgent catheterisation and bloods for dialysis planning.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **“A-B-C-D-E” of acute retention**  
  A – Age >60  
  B – BPH / bladder outlet obstruction  
  C – Constipation / catheter need  
  D – Drugs (anticholinergics, opioids)  
  E – Evident distension / Emergency

- **“P-S-S-S-T” for causes**  
  Prostate (BPH/cancer)  
  Stricture (urethral)  
  Stone (bladder)  
  Spinal (cord injury)  
  Tumour (pelvic)

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Always check a post-void residual in any elderly patient with “chronic UTI” or “incontinence”** – you may be treating overflow incontinence.
2. **In women with acute retention**, think of a pelvic mass, urethral diverticulum, or anti-cholinergic drugs – BPH is not the cause.
3. **In men <50 with retention**, suspect urethral stricture (from previous trauma or STI) or prostatitis.
4. **If you can’t catheterise with a 14Fr, try a 12Fr or 10Fr** – do not force. If still impossible, call urology or do suprapubic.
5. **Do not send a PSA in acute retention** – prostatic manipulation (catheter + infection) will falsely elevate it. Wait 2 weeks.
6. **In post-op patients**, schedule routine bladder scans if no void within 6 hours – early detection prevents prolonged distension.
7. **One hour of complete retention = one hour of bladder ischaemia** – the detrusor muscle can recover if decompressed within 12–24 h.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- **Giving fluids to “push urine out”** – this worsens distension and pain. Catheterise first.
- **Attempting a trial without catheter without starting an alpha-blocker first** in men with BPH – high failure rate.
- **Ignoring a high-output state post-catheter** – can lead to dangerous electrolyte derangements.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (registrar / consultant) / ICU / crash team:**

| Threshold | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Call medical registrar – sepsis / shock possible. |
| **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** | Suspect urosepsis – start IV antibiotics, escalate. |
| **GCS < 15 or dropping** | Sepsis-associated encephalopathy – consider ICU. |
| **Anuria despite catheter** (i.e., catheter in but no urine) | Catheter blocked? Bladder rupture? Renal failure? Call urology immediately. |
| **Unable to catheterise + patient in severe pain** | Call urology for suprapubic catheter. |
| **Post-catheter urine output > 400 mL/hour for >4 hours** | Call senior for fluid management plan – risk of post-obstructive diuresis. |
| **Suspected cauda equina syndrome** (saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, bladder numbness) | Emergency MRI spine + neurosurgery consult within 6 hours. |
| **Creatinine rise >50% from baseline** | AKI – need renal ultrasound + fluid balance review. |

**Remember:** If in doubt, catheterise. If still in doubt after catheter, call for help.

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Urinary Retention
**One-liner:** Sudden, painful inability to void with a palpable full bladder; most common in elderly men with BPH, but also post-op or neurogenic; missed cases cause renal damage.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man with known BPH presents to the Emergency Department with suprapubic pain and the sensation that he desperately needs to pass urine but cannot. He has not passed urine for 12 hours. On examination, his bladder is distended to the umbilicus and tender. A bladder scan shows 800 mL of urine. He is agitated and sweating.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age 72 + known BPH** → prostatic obstruction is the top cause.
- **Suprapubic pain + “need to go”** → bladder wall stretching activates stretch receptors; severe discomfort.
- **No void for 12 hours** → acute complete retention (as opposed to chronic).
- **Bladder distended to umbilicus** → palpable suprapubic mass; typical of >300 mL retained.
- **Bladder scan 800 mL** → confirms retention; post-void residual >300 mL is abnormal, >600 mL is severe.
- **Agitated, sweating** → autonomic response to pain and distension; also may signal high pressure.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Postoperative patient (e.g., hip replacement)**: After spinal anaesthesia/opioids, retention is painless because sensory blockade blunts the urge. The only clue is a distended bladder on exam or poor urine output. Always check a bladder scan in any post-op patient with low output.
2. **Neurogenic retention (e.g., diabetic autonomic neuropathy)**: Presents with chronic, painless distension and overflow incontinence. The patient may not complain of pain; instead they report constant dribbling. Exam reveals a nontender, large bladder. Post-void residual often >1 L.
3. **Medication-induced (e.g., anticholinergics in an older adult)**: Sudden onset after starting a new drug (e.g., oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressant). The patient may have no prior bladder symptoms. Suspect if retention coincides with drug start.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute urinary retention is the sudden inability to pass urine **despite a full bladder**. The bladder is distended and painful (or occasionally painless if neurogenic). It is a urological emergency.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Very common – ~10% of men over 70 experience acute retention within 5 years of BPH diagnosis. Also common post-surgery (up to 20% of hip/knee arthroplasties).
- **Stakes:** If left untreated → bladder ischemia → detrusor muscle damage → chronic retention → hydronephrosis → acute kidney injury (AKI) → permanent renal impairment.
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing anuria (no urine production) as retention (can’t void despite full bladder); catheterisation in anuria is harmful. Also missing post-obstructive diuresis which can cause life-threatening fluid/electrolyte shifts.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings
**The story of acute retention:**

1. **Obstruction (most common):** Bladder neck or urethra mechanically blocked – typically by an enlarged prostate (BPH) that compresses the prostatic urethra, like a kinked hose. Once the detrusor muscle can no longer overcome the resistance, it gives way → the bladder fails to empty.

2. **Detrusor failure (underactive bladder):** The bladder muscle (detrusor) lacks the strength or nerve supply to contract. Common in diabetics (autonomic neuropathy), post-anaesthesia (opioids relax smooth muscle), or after long-standing chronic retention (muscle stretched beyond its elastic limit).

3. **Neurological:** Spinal cord injury or cauda equina syndrome disrupts the sacral micturition reflex (S2–S4). The bladder becomes areflexic – no sensation of fullness, no ability to contract.

**Mechanism -> sign:**
- **Pain** → bladder wall hypoxia from severe distension stimulates visceral afferents (via pelvic nerve). Also, prostaglandins released by stretching trigger inflammation.
- **Palpable suprapubic mass** → bladder is a muscular bag that, when distended, rises out of the pelvis and becomes percussible/dull. A bladder volume of ~400 mL is palpable suprapubically; >600 mL is obvious.
- **Agitation / sweating** → pain and distension activate the sympathetic nervous system (fight or flight) – catecholamine surge.
- **Absence of void despite urge** → obstruction prevents flow; or detrusor cannot generate pressure.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
In the classic vignette, the patient has both obstruction and detrusor fatigue – his BPH (obstruction) eventually overcomes his detrusor strength. The pain tells you the bladder is still trying to contract (sensory arc intact). A painless distended bladder suggests neurogenic or chronic decompensation.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Obstructive** | BPH, urethral stricture, bladder stone, prostate cancer, pelvic mass, phimosis (children) | “Elderly man with prostatic enlargement”; “previous urethral trauma”; “palpable stone” |
| **Neurogenic** | Spinal cord injury, cauda equina syndrome, stroke, diabetes, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s | “Painless distension + overflow incontinence”; “reduced lower limb reflexes”; “saddle anaesthesia” |
| **Pharmacologic** | Anticholinergics (e.g., oxybutynin, tricyclics), alpha-agonists (decongestants, antihistamines), opioids, benzodiazepines, calcium channel blockers | “Started new medication recently”; “post-op opioids” |
| **Infectious / Inflammatory** | Acute prostatitis, cystitis, urethritis, balanitis | “Fever + tender prostate”; “dysuria before retention” |
| **Postoperative** | General/ spinal anaesthesia, opioids, supine position, immobility, pelvic surgery | “Day 1 after surgery – poor urine output”; “patient not yet mobilising” |
| **Other** | Severe constipation (compression), pelvic hematoma, trauma, psychogenic (rare) | “Has not opened bowels for days”; “post-fall pelvic fracture” |

**Most common cause overall:** **Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)** – accounts for ~60% of acute retention in men >60.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Acute retention is **clinical** – patient cannot void + painful bladder + residual volume >300 mL. There is no formal scoring system.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Bladder scan (post-void residual)** | Volume of urine remaining after void (if patient can void) | Confirm retention; quantify severity | >300 mL = abnormal; >600 mL = significant. If patient can’t void at all, scan immediately shows distended bladder (often >800 mL) |
| **Urinalysis** | Leukocytes, nitrites, blood | Rule out infection as cause or complication | +nitrites = acute prostatitis/UTI – treat before catheter removal |
| **Serum creatinine + U&E** | Renal function | Assess for AKI from back pressure / volume depletion | Elevated Cr suggests obstruction is causing hydronephrosis or pre-renal component. Post-obstructive diuresis can drop Cr rapidly |
| **Ultrasound kidney + bladder** | Hydronephrosis, bladder wall thickness, stones | If retention recurrent or unexplained; or AKI present | Bilateral hydronephrosis = high-pressure retention needing urgent decompression |
| **Prostate-specific antigen (PSA)** | Elevated in BPH/prostate cancer | Not routine in acute retention (can rise from infection/catheterisation); helpful later | Do not check within 48h of catheter – can be falsely high |
| **CT scan** | Pelvic masses, stones, ureteric obstruction | If suspicion of malignancy or stone causing retention | Look for bladder outlet or pelvic compression |

**Pattern recognition – single finding that rules in/out:**
- **Bladder scan >300 mL + inability to void** = confirms acute retention (rule in).
- **Bladder scan empty + patient says “can’t pee”** = anuria (renal failure or obstruction of both ureters). Do not catheterise – instead check renal ultrasound.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

#### Immediate (first hour) — “Decompress the bladder”
- **Urethral catheterisation**: 14–16Fr Foley, balloon 10 mL with water. Use lignocaine gel. Clean technique unless immunocompromised (then sterile).  
  **Why:** Relieves pain, prevents renal damage.  
  **Dose of lignocaine gel:** 11 mL 2% gel intraurethrally, wait 2–3 minutes.
- **If urethral catheter impossible** (e.g., known stricture, recent prostate surgery, patient on anticoagulants): **suprapubic catheterisation** under ultrasound guidance.  
  **Why:** Avoids urethral trauma.
- **Slow or fast decompression?** Older dogma said decompress slowly if volume >1 L to avoid haematuria or hypotension. Evidence shows **no difference** in outcomes – you can drain fully immediately. However, monitor for **post-obstructive diuresis** (see complications).  
  **Pitfall:** Do **not** clamp the catheter – that risks bladder rupture and infection.
- **Pain management**: Paracetamol 1g IV/PO; consider small doses of opioid if severe pain (but avoid exacerbating retention in future).

#### Ward (24–72 hours) — “Plan for catheter removal”
- **Trial Without Catheter (TWOC)** is attempted after 24–72 h **if** the cause is reversible (e.g., post-op, drug-induced).  
  **How:** Fill bladder with 300–400 mL sterile water via catheter, then remove catheter. Patient must void voluntarily. Measure voided volume + post-void residual. If residual <100 mL, success.
- **If TWOC fails or cause is obstruction (e.g., BPH):**  
  - Start **alpha-blocker**: **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily** (or Alfuzosin 10 mg PO once daily). These relax the bladder neck / prostatic smooth muscle and improve flow.  
  - Continue catheter for 3–7 days. Have patient attempt TWOC on medication.
- **If recurrent retention despite alpha-blocker** → refer to urology for **Transurethral Resection of Prostate (TURP)**.
- **If infection present** → treat with antibiotics according to local guidelines (e.g., Trimethoprim 200 mg BD PO for 7 days for uncomplicated UTI; for acute prostatitis, Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD PO × 14–28 days). **Do not remove catheter until infection cleared.**

#### Long-term — “Prevent recurrence”
- **Lifestyle**: reduce fluid intake before bed, void regularly, treat constipation.
- **Medical therapy**: continue tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily (or combination with dutasteride 0.5 mg daily if BPH large – 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor reduces prostate volume over 6–12 months).
- **Surgical**: TURP for refractory cases; or prostatectomy if large glands or malignancy.
- **For neurogenic retention**: clean intermittent self-catheterisation (CISC) 4–6 times daily, oxybutynin to reduce bladder spasms.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not remove the catheter without a plan** (no alpha-blocker, no assessment for UTI) – high chance of re-retention.
- **Do not give a fluid bolus for high urine output post-decompression** unless patient is hypotensive – post-obstructive diuresis is usually self-limiting; fluid alone can worsen polyuria.
- **Do not ignore a painless distended bladder** – it may be chronic overflow – still requires decompression to protect kidneys.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acute Urinary Retention | Anuria (Renal / Pre-renal) | Overflow Incontinence (Chronic Retention) | Functional Incontinence (e.g., immobility) |
|---------|-------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe suprapubic | None | Mild or none | None |
| **Bladder exam** | Distended, tender, dull | Empty | Distended, nontender, dull | Normal |
| **Urine output** | None despite urge | None (patient doesn’t feel full) | Dribbling small amounts frequently | Can void but can't reach toilet |
| **Bladder scan** | >300 mL | <50 mL | >300 mL (large residual) | Normal |
| **Post-void residual** | N/A (can’t void) | N/A | >300 mL | <100 mL |
| **Creatinine** | Normal or raised (AKI) | Usually raised | May be normal early, raised late | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Patient desperate to void + inability + palpable bladder | Patient not disoriented, bladder empty | Patient unaware of bladder distension, constant leak | Patient mobile but cognitively impaired |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)**
- **How it presents**: Rising creatinine, oliguria (confusingly, patient still can’t void), volume overload. Seen if retention >24–48 h.
- **Key signs**: Flank pain if hydronephrosis, leg oedema.
- **What to do**: Catheterise immediately. Monitor urine output hourly post-decompression.

**2. Post-obstructive Diuresis**
- **How it presents**: After catheterisation, huge urine output (up to 10 L/day) due to impaired concentrating ability of tubules from back-pressure.
- **Key signs**: Polyuria, risk of hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypernatraemia.
- **What to do**: Monitor I/O 4-hourly. Replace 50% of urine output with 0.45% saline + electrolytes if output >250 mL/h for 4 h. Usually self-limiting in 48 h. **Do not overreplace** – can perpetuate diuresis.

**3. Bladder Rupture (rare, <1%)**
- **How it presents**: Sudden severe suprapubic pain, inability to pass catheter, or blood at meatus after catheter insertion. Signs of peritonism.
- **Key signs**: Elevated bladder pressure >200 cmH2O. X-ray cystogram shows extravasation.
- **What to do**: Urgent urology consult. Usually requires surgical repair if intraperitoneal.

**4. Urosepsis**
- **How it presents**: Fever, hypotension, confusion in a patient with retention and UTI.
- **Key signs**: Positive blood cultures for gram-negative rods.
- **What to do**: Catheterise + IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS). Escalate to ICU if shock.

**Late presentation pattern:**  
A patient with undiagnosed chronic retention (painless, large bladder) eventually develops bilateral hydronephrosis, presenting with uraemia (nausea, confusion, pruritus) and a non-tender distended bladder. This is “high-pressure chronic retention” – needs urgent catheterisation and bloods for dialysis planning.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
- **“A-B-C-D-E” of acute retention**  
  A – Age >60  
  B – BPH / bladder outlet obstruction  
  C – Constipation / catheter need  
  D – Drugs (anticholinergics, opioids)  
  E – Evident distension / Emergency

- **“P-S-S-S-T” for causes**  
  Prostate (BPH/cancer)  
  Stricture (urethral)  
  Stone (bladder)  
  Spinal (cord injury)  
  Tumour (pelvic)

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Always check a post-void residual in any elderly patient with “chronic UTI” or “incontinence”** – you may be treating overflow incontinence.
2. **In women with acute retention**, think of a pelvic mass, urethral diverticulum, or anti-cholinergic drugs – BPH is not the cause.
3. **In men <50 with retention**, suspect urethral stricture (from previous trauma or STI) or prostatitis.
4. **If you can’t catheterise with a 14Fr, try a 12Fr or 10Fr** – do not force. If still impossible, call urology or do suprapubic.
5. **Do not send a PSA in acute retention** – prostatic manipulation (catheter + infection) will falsely elevate it. Wait 2 weeks.
6. **In post-op patients**, schedule routine bladder scans if no void within 6 hours – early detection prevents prolonged distension.
7. **One hour of complete retention = one hour of bladder ischaemia** – the detrusor muscle can recover if decompressed within 12–24 h.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- **Giving fluids to “push urine out”** – this worsens distension and pain. Catheterise first.
- **Attempting a trial without catheter without starting an alpha-blocker first** in men with BPH – high failure rate.
- **Ignoring a high-output state post-catheter** – can lead to dangerous electrolyte derangements.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (registrar / consultant) / ICU / crash team:**

| Threshold | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Call medical registrar – sepsis / shock possible. |
| **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** | Suspect urosepsis – start IV antibiotics, escalate. |
| **GCS < 15 or dropping** | Sepsis-associated encephalopathy – consider ICU. |
| **Anuria despite catheter** (i.e., catheter in but no urine) | Catheter blocked? Bladder rupture? Renal failure? Call urology immediately. |
| **Unable to catheterise + patient in severe pain** | Call urology for suprapubic catheter. |
| **Post-catheter urine output > 400 mL/hour for >4 hours** | Call senior for fluid management plan – risk of post-obstructive diuresis. |
| **Suspected cauda equina syndrome** (saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, bladder numbness) | Emergency MRI spine + neurosurgery consult within 6 hours. |
| **Creatinine rise >50% from baseline** | AKI – need renal ultrasound + fluid balance review. |

**Remember:** If in doubt, catheterise. If still in doubt after catheter, call for help.
